questions.questionText: Fatty acids contain a hydrophobic hydrocarbon chain and a:
questions.explanation: Fatty acids have a long nonpolar hydrocarbon chain and a polar carboxyl group. At physiological pH, the carboxyl group is usually ionized, but long-chain fatty acids are still mainly hydrophobic because the hydrocarbon chain dominates.

questions.questionText: Long-chain fatty acids are transported in plasma mainly bound to:
questions.explanation: Long-chain fatty acids are poorly soluble in water. Therefore, free fatty acids released into blood are transported bound to serum albumin.

questions.questionText: Fatty acids may be classified according to saturation into:
options: Acidic and basic fatty acids only
Ketogenic and glucogenic fatty acids only
questions.explanation: Saturated fatty acids have no double bonds, while unsaturated fatty acids contain one or more double bonds.

questions.questionText: A saturated fatty acid contains:
questions.explanation: Saturated fatty acids contain only single bonds between carbon atoms in their hydrocarbon chain.

questions.questionText: An unsaturated fatty acid contains:
questions.explanation: Unsaturated fatty acids contain at least one carbon-carbon double bond. Polyunsaturated fatty acids contain two or more double bonds.

questions.questionText: Essential fatty acids are fatty acids that:
options: Cannot be synthesized by the body and must be supplied by diet
questions.explanation: Essential fatty acids must be obtained from the diet because the human body cannot synthesize them in sufficient amounts.

questions.questionText: Linoleic acid is classified as:
questions.explanation: Linoleic acid is an essential fatty acid and must be obtained from the diet. It can also serve as a precursor for other polyunsaturated fatty acids.

questions.questionText: α-Linolenic acid is classified as:
options: A saturated fatty acid made only in liver
questions.explanation: α-Linolenic acid is an essential fatty acid. It must be supplied by diet because humans cannot introduce double bonds at the required positions.

questions.questionText: Arachidonic acid is considered essential mainly when the diet lacks:
questions.explanation: Arachidonic acid can be synthesized from linoleic acid by elongation and desaturation. Therefore, it becomes essential if linoleic acid is absent from the diet.

questions.questionText: Saturated fatty acids are relatively:
options: Easily oxidized in air compared with unsaturated fatty acids
questions.explanation: Saturated fatty acids are more resistant to spontaneous oxidation. Unsaturated fatty acids are more easily oxidized in air, which can lead to rancidity.

questions.questionText: Unsaturated fatty acids help maintain membrane:
questions.explanation: Double bonds introduce bends in fatty acid chains. This reduces tight packing and helps maintain the fluid nature of biological membranes.

questions.questionText: Increasing fatty acid chain length generally:
options: Has no effect on melting temperature
questions.explanation: Longer fatty acid chains have stronger hydrophobic interactions, so their melting temperature increases.

questions.questionText: Adding double bonds to a fatty acid generally:
options: Makes it unable to form lipids
questions.explanation: Double bonds disrupt tight packing of fatty acid chains, lowering the melting temperature and increasing fluidity.

questions.questionText: Most fatty acids used by the body are supplied by:
questions.explanation: The file states that most fatty acids used by the body come from the diet. However, excess carbohydrate and protein can also be converted into fatty acids.

questions.questionText: Excess carbohydrates and proteins can be converted into:
questions.explanation: When energy intake exceeds immediate needs, carbon from carbohydrates and proteins can be converted to fatty acids and stored as triacylglycerol.

questions.questionText: De novo fatty acid synthesis occurs mainly in:
questions.explanation: In humans, fatty acid synthesis occurs mainly in the liver and lactating mammary glands. These tissues are active in lipid biosynthesis.

questions.questionText: Fatty acid synthesis occurs primarily in the:
questions.explanation: Fatty acid synthesis is mainly cytosolic. This contrasts with fatty acid degradation, which occurs mainly in mitochondria.

questions.questionText: The building unit for fatty acid synthesis is derived from:
questions.explanation: Fatty acid synthesis incorporates carbons from acetyl-CoA into the growing fatty acid chain.

questions.questionText: Acetyl-CoA is transported from mitochondria to cytosol mainly as:
questions.explanation: Acetyl-CoA cannot cross the inner mitochondrial membrane directly. It is exported to the cytosol as citrate, then cleaved to regenerate acetyl-CoA.

questions.questionText: Fatty acid synthesis requires reducing power mainly in the form of:
questions.explanation: NADPH provides the reducing equivalents needed for fatty acid synthesis.

questions.questionText: The major source of NADPH for fatty acid synthesis is:
questions.explanation: The HMP shunt, also called the pentose phosphate pathway, is the major source of NADPH required for fatty acid synthesis.

questions.questionText: Another source of NADPH for fatty acid synthesis is:
questions.explanation: Malic enzyme, a cytosolic NADP⁺-dependent malate dehydrogenase, can produce NADPH and support fatty acid synthesis.

questions.questionText: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase converts acetyl-CoA into:
questions.explanation: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase catalyzes the carboxylation of acetyl-CoA to malonyl-CoA, an important committed step in fatty acid synthesis.

questions.questionText: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase requires which vitamin-derived coenzyme?
questions.explanation: Acetyl-CoA carboxylase is a carboxylase enzyme. Biotin acts as a CO₂ carrier in carboxylation reactions.

questions.questionText: Malonyl-CoA is important in fatty acid synthesis because it is:
options: The two-carbon donor used for chain elongation
The main carrier of dietary cholesterol
questions.explanation: Malonyl-CoA donates two-carbon units during fatty acid synthesis. It is formed from acetyl-CoA by acetyl-CoA carboxylase.

questions.questionText: The final product of normal de novo fatty acid synthesis is mainly:
questions.explanation: The major product of fatty acid synthase is palmitate, a 16-carbon saturated fatty acid.

questions.questionText: Triacylglycerols are also called:
questions.explanation: Triacylglycerols are commonly called triglycerides. They are made of glycerol esterified with three fatty acids.

questions.questionText: Triacylglycerols are:
questions.explanation: Triacylglycerols are neutral lipids. They are highly hydrophobic and coalesce inside adipocytes as oily droplets.

questions.questionText: The major energy storage form in adipose tissue is:
questions.explanation: Triacylglycerol is the major stored energy form in adipose tissue. It stores more energy per gram than carbohydrate or protein.

questions.questionText: Triacylglycerol synthesis occurs mainly in:
questions.explanation: Triacylglycerol synthesis occurs primarily in the liver and adipose tissue. Liver can export TG as VLDL, while adipose tissue stores TG.
